params.text:
The duty to make complaining easy

The regulations require every service to have a complaints procedure that is clear, published and genuinely accessible. CQC Regulation 16 expects providers to operate an effective system for identifying, receiving, recording, handling and responding to complaints. It is not enough to have a policy in a folder — people must actually know how to complain and feel able to.

  • Tell people how to complain, who to, and what will happen next.
  • Provide information in formats and languages people can understand — easy read, large print, translated, or spoken.
  • Offer support to complain, including access to an advocate for someone who would struggle to do so alone.
  • Make sure no one fears that complaining will affect the care they receive.

A barrier-free culture

Accessibility is about attitude as much as paperwork. People may worry they will be seen as a nuisance, or that speaking up will lead to worse care. A good service actively reassures them that the opposite is true: raising a concern is welcomed, and it will never count against them. When people trust that, they tell us about problems while they are still small.

params.question: Which of these help make a complaints procedure genuinely accessible?

answers.text: Offering the information in easy read, large print and other languages.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Information must be available in formats and languages that meet people’s needs.

answers.text: Providing an advocate for someone who would find it hard to complain alone.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Support to complain, including advocacy, is part of an accessible system.

answers.text: Only accepting complaints if they are put in a formal letter.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Insisting on one rigid format is a barrier; complaints can be made in many ways.

answers.text: Reassuring people that complaining will never affect their care.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Removing fear of reprisal is essential to a truly accessible culture.
